# Brodie McCartan COMP 110 Final Project
## An Analysis of Course Survey Data and its Relation to Course Improvement Suggestions

This website addresses my idea that COMP 110 lectures should contain more examples of basic python coding principles to account for the portion of students that go into with little to no coding experience. Here, I create and analyze various data visuals from our course survey, looking at how many students enter the course with limited experience, as well as how experience relates to ratings of lecture effectiveness and understanding. 

## Data Visualization

Figure 3: Bar graph with error bars showing the mean rating of lecture effectiveness by experience level.

## Analysis of Data

Through looking at my data representations and connecting them to the idea I wanted to address, I have found my data to be inconclusive. The first visual I decided to use to analyze the survey data was a collection of scatterplots of understanding versus own_example ratings by experience level with a trendline for each. Here, I believed my idea would be supported if a steeper positive slope was observed in the trendlines in scatterplots for students with less experience. I wanted to determine whether students with less coding experience saw a greater increase in understanding of content when they were able to apply more of their own examples. Since a change in prior experience seems to have a limited impact on these slopes, I cannot draw any conclusions from these graphs.

I was also hoping to gauge the percentage of students in the class with no coding experience, which I did in my first bar graph. Though knowing the number of students with low prior experience would not fully support or refute my idea, showing that a large number of students go into the class with no coding experience would show a high stakeholder value if my other two visualizations were conclusive in my favor. My bar graph did show this with the majority of students having none to less than a month of prior experience. However, this alone does not make my results conclusive as I would also need to show a relationship between prior experience and another variable in the survey for this visual to support my idea.

For my third visual, I hoped to find whether lecture effectiveness rating varied with experience level. Since my idea proposes that time is set aside during lecture specifically to review basic python coding examples, these results were important. I also decided to include error bars in my visual in case a significant difference needed to shown between average lecture effectiveness ratings of any two groups. With this taken into account, I found no significant differences in average lecture effectiveness ratings between all of the groups. Because of this, these results are inconclusive as well.

Two variables I think I could have factored into my data analysis to support my idea are oh_visits and oh_effective. It is possible that students with less programming experience are more likely to visit office hours and discuss specific examples of python code there, creating less of a need for additional instruction in class. I believe further analysis of this would be insightful, even if it does not support my idea. One drawback of my idea is it could force the instructional staff to have to rush through important course content in order to plan for setting aside the time suggested in my idea. This could also negatively impact enrolled students who are already familiar with the basics of python, potentially providing them with redundant information. A solution to this could be implementing my idea once a week rather than every lecture.
